utilityName: City of College Station Backflow Prevention
testingFrequency: Installation testing plus ordinance-driven ongoing reporting
dueBasis: College Station says backflow prevention devices must be tested on installation, testers must be TCEQ-certified and registered with the City, and original reports must reach Water Services within 30 days of testing per city ordinance.
coveredPropertyTypes: Lawn irrigation systems requiring a permit and approved backflow device
Properties where a certified inspector deems a backflow assembly appropriate
Sites requiring general or fireline registered testers
coveredDeviceTypes: Fireline and general assemblies represented on the City's tester list
officialListLabel: Open the College Station registered tester list

submissionMethods.label: College Station drinking water and backflow page

penalties: College Station requires tester registration with the City and says original reports must reach Water Services within 30 days of testing. Tester registration also carries a $50 annual company fee.
sourceExcerpt: College Station is strong on source-backed process: install-test requirement, city-registered BPATs, a published tester list, a report form, and a 30-day report delivery rule tied to ordinance.
verdictSummary: College Station is a useful procedural page because it clearly publishes the tester list, report form, 30-day submission rule, and City registration requirements for BPATs.
whoIsAffected: Lawn irrigation systems, properties where inspectors require a backflow assembly, and fireline or general assemblies using City-registered testers.
residentialNotes: Residential users most often see College Station through irrigation permit work and required installation testing.
City registration still matters on the tester side even when the property is smaller.
commercialNotes: Commercial and managed properties should pay attention to the separate fireline and general tester categories on the City list.
The report deadline and company registration fee make College Station more process-heavy than a generic plumber explanation suggests.

annualTesting.summary: College Station is strongest on process rather than consumer copy: the City publishes the tester list, report form, and a 30-day report delivery rule.
highlights: The test report must reach Water Services within 30 days of testing.
The tester must be both TCEQ certified and registered with the City.
The City publishes separate fireline and general tester lists in one PDF.
workflowSteps: Deliver the original report to Water Services within 30 days.

irrigation.summary: College Station explicitly ties irrigation systems to permitting and approved backflow devices, which makes this a clear irrigation compliance page.
highlights: All lawn irrigation systems must have an installation permit and an approved backflow prevention device.
The irrigation workflow sits next to the same City tester registration and report rules.
workflowSteps: Complete the irrigation permit path first.
Test the device on installation using a City-registered BPAT.
Send the original report to Water Services within 30 days.

fireLine.summary: College Station's published tester list is useful for fire line cases because it separates fireline testers from general testers.
highlights: The City list distinguishes fireline testers from general testers.
Using the wrong tester category creates avoidable compliance risk.
The same report and delivery rules still apply once the right tester is chosen.
workflowSteps: Confirm the device falls into the fireline side of the tester list.
Use a registered fireline tester rather than assuming a general tester is enough.
Submit the report through the City process within the ordinance window.
Confirm whether the project or property requires a backflow assembly through Water Services or the irrigation permit workflow.
Use a TCEQ-certified BPAT who is also registered with the City of College Station.
Complete the City report form and deliver the original report to Water Services within 30 days of testing.
If you need a tester, start with the City's registered tester PDF rather than a generic search.
failureHighlights: College Station separates state certification from City registration; both matter.
The report deadline is tied to when testing occurs, not just when a notice is received.
Irrigation work adds a permit requirement on top of the testing workflow.

costBand.testingRange: The City does not publish consumer testing price bands, so retail cost remains market-based.
costBand.repairRetestRange: Repair and retest price depends on the assembly and contractor; City materials focus on procedure rather than consumer pricing.
costBand.pricingNotes: The published $50 annual registration fee applies to tester companies, not directly to the property owner.

sources.label: College Station drinking water and backflow page
